title: Supramolecular order in new polymer electrolytes
abstract:
Polymer International 47 (1998) 34È42
Supramolecular Order in New Polymer
Electrolytes
Peter V. Wright,1,* Y. Zheng,1 D. Bhatt,2 T. Richardson2 & G. Ungar1
1 Department of Engineering Materials, Centre for Molecular Materials, University of Sheffield, Mappin Street,

2 Department of Physics, University of Sheffield, HoundsÐeld Road, Sheffield, UK
(Received 4 November 1997; accepted 21 January 1998)
Abstract: The structure and conductivity of crystalline, semicrystalline and
amorphous complexes of poly(ethylene oxide) with sodium and lithium salts is
brieÑy reviewed and the rationale for synthesizing low-dimensional, organized
polyether complexes is discussed. New polymer electrolytes derived from:

organized systems. LangmuirÈBlodgett Ðlms of the complexes (20 molecular
layers) are readily deposited. They show temperature-independence of conduc-
tivity over the range 10¡C to about 90¡C.

INTRODUCTION
It is well understood that in “conventionalÏ, solvent-free
polymer electrolytes based upon poly(ethylene oxide)
(PEO) ion mobility is optimized in amorphous phases.

segments2h4 and seemingly conÐrmed by the elegant
NMR investigations of Berthier et al.5 The relatively

case. It is this realization that prompts the search for
new directions in the strategic approach to improved
conductivities in solvent-free polymer electrolytes.

tures clearly incorporate channels between and within

with little impediment. The fact that ions are less mobile
than in their corresponding amorphous phases must, to
a signiÐcant degree, arise from the “gridlockÏ imposed by
the strong coulombic lattice interactions and low
vacancy concentrations. Lattice energy calculations12,13
for the PEOÈNaI system shown in Fig. 3 provide a
rationale for complex formation if the partial charge on
the carbon atoms of the polyether is 0.23(]) of the elec-
tronic charge. However, the calculations also reveal that
the intrahelical contribution to the lattice energy is sub-
stantially larger than that of the interhelical one. Such a
system, consisting of stable molecular adducts separated
by weak intermolecular forces, might be expected to
display mesogenic behaviour above a premelting tran-
sition during which the interchain organization is lost.
This speculation is supported by the onset of 1H NMR
line-narrowing3 and the simultaneous decline in mecha-
nical rigidity12 in PEOÈNaI at about 60¡C (see Fig. 4),
whilst any changes in the spherulitic texture below the

dimensional amorphous systems might be anticipated
for polymer electrolytes of low dimensionality.
(1) The movement of ions through channels of planar
cavities, unimpeded by a random coil network, should
involve a lesser degree of coupling between the relax-
ations of the supporting “molecular sca†oldingÏ and the
mobilities of ions. In amorphous systems ion mobility is
motions that become accessible at the glass transition
temperature. In a low dimensional system, some inter-
action between at least one of the ionic species and its
polymer environment will be required to stabilize the
salt in-phase, but translation between closely spaced
coordinating atoms may not require the displacement of
substantial polymer segments in a suitably organized
(2) The dispersion of the salt in one- or two-
dimensional ionic arrays of one or two molecular layers
thickness should inhibit the formation of ionic aggre-
larger aggregates) which, within a medium of low dielec-
tric constant, give rise to low concentrations of overall
charged species. In amorphous systems ion aggregates
will be stabilized by freedom in three-dimensions to
engage in complex exchange equilibria with more disso-
ciated species. ConÐnement within channels or tubes
should tend to limit the sizes of aggregates that are
likely to be stable under the prevailing thermodynamic
conditions. The same case is e†ectively made by point-
ing to the lower stabilities of surface ions in relation to
their counterparts in the sublattice.
(3) Steric modiÐcation of the helical structure may
bring about some desirable degree of destabilization of
the ions in their local environments within and without
the helices. As Fig. 2 shows, in the helical P(EO)3ÈMA
complexes the ions are arranged in a well-deÐned
cationÈanionÈcationÈanion “zig-zagÏ and the intrahelical
component of the lattice energies (Fig. 3) conÐrms the
essential stability of this conÐguration. (It seems that
ionic species when constrained in low-dimensional
arrays may inevitably opt for this arrangement, as was
also pointed out for amphiphilic ionic smectic mesogens
Supramolecular order in new polymer electrolytes

by Skoulios and Luzzati14). Some perturbation of the

However, the imposition of low-dimensional order on
the system clearly carries with it the constraint of ensur-
ing that the conducting pathways are accessible to the
electrodes connecting the outer circuit. Orientations of
conducting planes for a two-dimensional system in
which one dimension of the plane lies along the orient-
ed chains (in the plane of the Ðlm) is shown in Fig. 5. In
Fig. 5(a) the conducting planes are favourably oriented
between the electrodes (although there may be anisot-
ropy of conduction in this plane). In Fig. 5(b) the orien-
tation of planes is clearly unfavourable for electrodes
mounted normal to the plane of the Ðlm. The realization
of either of these orientations throughout the system
will require suitable mechanisms of self- or imposed
organized material into a microcrystalline, granular or
“micellarÏ morphology will result in the “pseudo three-
dimensionalÏ system of Fig. 5(c) in which some advan-
tages of low-dimensional systems are retained, but
pathways are necessarily longer and more tortuous and
intermicellar barriers to mobility will be encountered.
In attempting to reduce the dimensionality the PEOÈ
alkali salt system investigations15h17 have been made of

were further dried at 120¡C and melted into a cell of
indiumÈtin oxide glass electrodes with a cellulose
acetate spacer (60 lm) enabling the “laminarÏ morphol-
ogy of the sheared samples to be monitored by hot-
stage polarized light microscopy. The material was
sheared by displacing the glass slides at about 45¡C and
clamping them together in the measuring rig before
applying a vacuum. Complex impedance measurements,
X-ray scattering, di†erential scanning calorimetry and
hot-stage polarized light microscopy were performed as
X-ray scattering, DSC, hot-stage polarized light micros-

pure C16O5 is organized in a laminar structure with
interdigitating alkyl chains as shown in Fig. 6(a).

molecular origin of the higher transition is not yet clear,
but it may involve the disorganization of the helical
molecular ranks and associated “ion-looseningÏ as dis-
cussed above in the PEO complexes. In neither system
is the transition apparent in DSC traces. However, the
helical separation required for such a process in the
C16O5 complexes should be facilitated by the higher
dielectric constant within the locality of the interhelical
ion-containing plane that the presence of the hydroxyl
group should bring about (see Fig. 6d). The absence of
the higher temperature transition on cooling the wOH-
containing system is consistent with some screening of
the coulombic forces of reordering. It suggests the possi-
bility that this local reorganization may be inhibited by
modiÐcation of the molecular structure of the system
whilst the supramolecular organization is retained. This
would allow extrapolation of the higher temperature,
liquid crystal region of the conductivity plot to give
enhanced conductivities at lower temperatures, a strat-
egy reminiscent of the analogous process of inhibition
of crystallinity in PEO-based systems4 (Fig. 1, curve d).

[1 : 1].) The persistence of some order
at these temperatures is shown in the low-angle X-ray
data (Fig. 9) and is supported by complex impedance
plots17 in which the high frequency arc (f O 65 kHz)
persists in part, denoting a greater capacitative contri-
bution than is normally observed in fully amorphous
peratures the materials may adopt micellar microstruc-
tures as depicted schematically in Fig. 5(c). The essential
feature of this structure, promoted by orientation and
thermal treatment, is the proliferation of continuous
pathways of the ionophilic material and a minimization
of the inverse structure in which the continuum is ion-
ophobic. The latter would create non-conductive regions
with enclosed pockets of ions. The relative proportions
of ionophilic and ionophobic continua in a given
sample would be a major factor in determining conduc-
The most extensively organized examples of these
molecular complexes are possibly those deposited by
LangmuirÈBlodgett (LB) procedures, because the iono-
philic and ionophobic portions of the molecules are per-
fectly segregated throughout the molecular length on
the surface of the aqueous subphase. LB complexed
Ðlms may be prepared by either adding salt to the sub-
phase in signiÐcant concentration (about 1 molar) fol-
lowed by dispersion of the trace quantity of the pure
C16O5 solution onto the surface18,19 or by spreading
trace quantities of a chloroform solution of the per-
formed complex onto the aqueous surface19,20 (see Fig.
13). The latter method seems to ensure the more well-
organized LB Ðlms.19 (This is perhaps surprising con-
sidering the possibility of dispersing the trace quantity
of the salt into the aqueous subphase.) Monolayers of
and also with salts of the more
(“triÑateÏ) and perÑuorophenate may be deposited in this
way. The uptake, or retention of salt by the Langmuir
Ðlms is evident from their surface areas per repeat unit
which increase in the sequence
C16O5 : C20H41NH2 : NaCF3SO3 ,
The salt may also be detected by grazing incidence
FTIR in the deposited LangmuirÈBlodgett Ðlms.19,20
However, its presence in the Ðlms is strikingly demon-
strated by the conductivity data for 20 molecular layers
(perÑuorophenate) shown in Fig. 14. In these measure-
ments the electrodes lie above and below the Ðlms and
so measure conductivities normal to the planes of facile
ion displacement, as schematically shown in Fig. 5(b).
This clearly represents the least conductive arrangement
of electrodes and Ðlm. Nonetheless, the salt-containing
system is 10 orders of magnitude more conductive than
the pure polymer at 20¡C and Ðve orders more conduc-
tive at 60¡C. At ambient temperature the conductivity
exceeds that of the poorly-organized bulk sample.

However, the most remarkable aspect of the conductiv-
ity of the LB samples is the apparent temperature-
independence which extends over the approximate range
10È90¡C. The decline in conductivity at temperatures
above 90¡C presumably arises from disruption of the
conducting pathways following lateral thermal expan-
The temperature-independence of conductivity in the
LB Ðlms is intriguing and appears to be a general phe-
nomenon in all preformed complex Ðlms which have
been studied so far. It is tempting to speculate that it
may reÑect facile ion mobility along the unimpeded
channels and helices of perfectly organized laminar
material. The substrate should impose a constraint
However, the layers must conform to the topology of
the electrode substrate which is unlikely to be perfectly
Ñat. The topology may thus give rise to a component of
the conductivity perpendicular to the substrate. Con-
ductivity measurements in the planes of the LB Ðlms
should shed more light on the conduction mechanism
Comparison of Figs 10È12 with Fig. 1, curves a and b
capable of exceeding the conductivities of conventional

Higher ambient conductivities would seem to require
the side chain melting temperature. An
conductivity of the LB Ðlms is its implication for con-
ductivity in bulk Ðlms if appropriate orientation and
perfection of structure can be realized. The organization
achieved at the waterÈair interface of the Langmuir
Ðlms suggests that low-molecular solvents may be used
to impose organization on bulk materials as emulsions
before being removed by volatilization. Polar solvents,
such as water or alcohols, would presumably give rise
to a continuum of ionophilic material, in analogy with
oil-in-water emulsions, and promote ionic mobility over
long range in the material. The inverse, ionophobic con-
tinuum should arise from the use of non-polar solvents
in analogy with water-in-oil emulsions. The latter
should restrict the range of ion mobility, with the possi-
bility of high capacitance at the internal surfaces of the
materials, suggesting their use as dielectrics. These
aspects of correlation between electrical properties and
the nature of the deposition solvents are under investi-
